title: Mixed LF interface/contract versions cause serialization inconsistency
description: Mixed LF interface/contract versions (LF 1.15 interface on LF 1.17 contracts) cause serialization and key hashing inconsistency. When an interface defined in one LF version is used on contracts compiled with a different LF version, the serialization format and contract key hashing can differ, leading to subtle correctness bugs where the same contract may produce different hashes depending on the code path.
recommendation: Ensure consistent LF versioning across interfaces and contracts. Canton 2.10.1 adds validation to reject mixed-version combinations that cause serialization inconsistency.

oracle.description: Interface choice invocations must produce consistent serialization regardless of the LF version combination of interface and contract packages.
oracle.static_signal: Detect mixed LF version usage between interface definitions and implementing contracts
oracle.symbolic_property: forall i c. interface_lf_version(i) != contract_lf_version(c) => serialize(invoke(i, c)) == serialize_expected(i, c)

notes: This is a Canton runtime bug, not a DAML contract-level bug. Relevant as a benchmark for package/upgrade validation tools. | Marked out-of-scope: runtime-layer bug, not detectable via contract-level static analysis or symbolic execution.

title: RepairService ACS export/import contract key inconsistency
description: RepairService incorrectly reuses input contract keys during ACS export/import, causing the key store and ACS to become inconsistent. After an export-import cycle, contract key lookups may return stale or incorrect results, breaking applications that rely on key uniqueness for state management.
recommendation: Upgrade to Canton 2.9.7+ which fixes the RepairService to properly handle contract keys during ACS migration.

oracle.description: ACS export/import must preserve contract key uniqueness and store consistency.
oracle.static_signal: Contract key reuse detection during import operations
oracle.symbolic_property: forall k. after_import(acs) => lookup_key(k) == expected_contract(k)

notes: Canton infrastructure bug affecting migration tooling. Demonstrates importance of key consistency invariants. | Marked out-of-scope: runtime-layer bug, not detectable via contract-level static analysis or symbolic execution.

title: Confidential config fields logged in plain text
description: Confidential configuration fields (e.g., database passwords, API keys) may be logged in plain text under specific config substitution/override patterns. When configuration values are resolved through environment variable substitution or override chains, the logging framework may serialize the resolved (secret) values rather than the masked placeholders.
recommendation: Upgrade to Canton 2.10.1 which masks sensitive configuration fields before logging. Review existing log output for exposed secrets.

oracle.description: Configuration values marked as sensitive must never appear in log output in plain text.
oracle.static_signal: Log statements that serialize config objects without field-level masking
oracle.symbolic_property: forall field. is_sensitive(field) => not(appears_in_log(resolve(field)))

notes: Not a DAML contract bug but an execution environment security issue. Relevant for operational security benchmarking. | Marked out-of-scope: runtime-layer bug, not detectable via contract-level static analysis or symbolic execution.

title: ACS export and party replication broken after hard domain migration
target_object: ACS export / party replication / domain migration
description: ACS export and party replication become non-functional after a hard domain migration. The migration process leaves internal state references pointing to the old domain, causing subsequent ACS export operations and party replication to fail silently or produce incomplete results.
recommendation: Upgrade to Canton 2.10.0 which fixes domain migration to properly update all internal state references.

oracle.description: Domain migration must update all internal references so that ACS export and party replication continue to function.
oracle.static_signal: State references not updated during domain migration
oracle.symbolic_property: forall ref. after_migration(domain) => resolve(ref) == valid_target(ref)

notes: Canton infrastructure bug. More relevant for runtime/migration safety tooling than contract-level analysis. | Marked out-of-scope: runtime-layer bug, not detectable via contract-level static analysis or symbolic execution.

title: Mixed LF packages cause INVALID_ARGUMENT or unexpected model behavior
description: Mixed LF packages uploaded to a participant may cause INVALID_ARGUMENT errors or unexpected model behavior at runtime. Before 2.10.1, there was no startup-time validation to detect incompatible package combinations. The flag unsafe-disable-upgrade-validation=true could bypass remaining guardrails. Daml 2.10.1 adds startup-time package compatibility validation and documents the risks of disabling upgrade validation.
recommendation: Upgrade to Daml 2.10.1 for startup-time package compatibility validation. Do not use unsafe-disable-upgrade-validation=true in production.

oracle.description: All uploaded packages must be validated for mutual LF version compatibility at startup time.
oracle.static_signal: Detection of mixed LF version packages in the same participant
oracle.symbolic_property: forall p1 p2. uploaded(p1) && uploaded(p2) => lf_compatible(p1, p2)

notes: Daml SDK-level bug. Related to CANTON-REL-01. Demonstrates need for package-level compatibility analysis tools. | Marked out-of-scope: runtime-layer bug, not detectable via contract-level static analysis or symbolic execution.

title: Missing role authorization check on privileged operation
source_title: DAML Authorization Pattern - Role-Based Access Control
description: A privileged operation (BurnFrom) is missing a role membership check. Any party authorized as controller can burn tokens from any account without holding the BURNER_ROLE. This is the most fundamental DAML authorization anti-pattern.
recommendation: Always verify role membership (Set.member caller roleSet) before executing privileged operations.

oracle.description: Privileged choices must validate caller role membership before execution.
oracle.static_signal: Choice body lacks assertMsg/assertRole on role set membership for controller
oracle.symbolic_property: forall caller. executes(BurnFrom, caller) => member(caller, roles.burners)

notes: Synthetic example distilled from common patterns seen in and other DAML audits.

title: Audit event with actor as signatory allows evidence deletion
source_title: DAML Diagnostics Pattern - Audit Record Integrity
description: An audit entry template has the acting party as signatory. In DAML, signatories can unilaterally archive contracts they have signed. This means the actor can delete their own audit records, defeating the purpose of the audit trail.
recommendation: Make the admin/governor the signatory of audit records, and the actor an observer. This prevents actors from deleting evidence of their own actions.

oracle.description: Audit records must not be archivable by the party whose actions they record.
oracle.static_signal: Audit/event template has the acting party as sole signatory
oracle.symbolic_property: forall e. audit_record(e) => not(can_archive(e.actor, e))

notes: Common DAML security pattern.

title: Missing expected-admin validation in factory proxy
description: A proxy template delegates to an underlying factory without verifying that the expected admin matches the actual admin. A caller could pass a mismatched expectedAdmin, potentially redirecting the operation to a different factory or bypassing admin-specific restrictions.
recommendation: Always verify expectedAdmin == admin before delegating to external factory contracts.

oracle.description: Proxy/delegation patterns must verify the expected admin matches before forwarding.
oracle.static_signal: expectedAdmin parameter not compared to template admin field
oracle.symbolic_property: forall proxy. delegates(proxy, factory) => verified(proxy.admin == expectedAdmin)

notes: Mirrors the requireExpectedAdminMatch pattern used throughout .

title: Asymmetric validation between paired execute and rollback paths
description: Two paired code paths (Execute and Rollback) that should maintain the same invariants apply different validation. Execute checks amount consistency; Rollback does not. This asymmetry can mask bugs or allow subtle state inconsistencies.
recommendation: Apply the same set of validation assertions to both the happy path and the error/rollback path.

oracle.description: Paired choices (execute/rollback, commit/abort) must validate the same set of invariants.
oracle.static_signal: Paired choices have different assertion sets
oracle.symbolic_property: assertions(Execute) ⊆ assertions(Rollback) && assertions(Rollback) ⊆ assertions(Execute)

notes: Common DAML security pattern.

title: Orphaned contract reference after standalone archive
description: A LockedAsset can be independently archived (via ExpireLock), but the TransferOrder holding a ContractId reference to it is not atomically updated. After ExpireLock, the TransferOrder has a dangling reference and its Execute choice will fail at fetch.
recommendation: Restrict archive paths so that referenced contracts can only be consumed through the referencing workflow (Execute/Cancel), not independently.

oracle.description: Contracts referenced by ContractId from other active contracts must not be independently archivable.
oracle.static_signal: Template has standalone archive choice while being referenced by ContractId in another template
oracle.symbolic_property: forall a b. references(b, a) && active(b) => not(archivable_independently(a))

notes: Fundamental DAML anti-pattern.

title: Unbounded observer set causes linear contract growth
source_title: DAML Scalability Pattern - Observer Set Management
description: A singleton registry template accumulates parties in a Set field that is used in the observer clause. As the set grows, every transaction touching the registry must include O(n) parties in the disclosure witness, eventually exceeding Canton's maximum transaction size.
recommendation: Use per-party child contracts instead of accumulating parties in a singleton set. This keeps the admin contract constant-size.

oracle.description: Template fields used in observer clauses must have bounded growth.
oracle.static_signal: Set/List field used in observer clause with insert but no size bound check
oracle.symbolic_property: forall n. after_n_registers(n) => contract_size(registry) <= constant

notes: Common architecture anti-pattern in DAML.

title: Missing deadline enforcement on time-sensitive choice
description: A time-sensitive choice (Vote) does not check whether the current time is within the allowed window. Votes can be cast after the deadline has passed, subverting the tally result.
recommendation: Add getTime + assertMsg check at the beginning of time-sensitive choices to enforce the deadline.

oracle.description: Time-sensitive choices must validate current time against their deadline.
oracle.static_signal: Choice body references Time field but lacks getTime + comparison assertion
oracle.symbolic_property: forall t. executes(Vote, t) => t < deadline

notes: Common in any workflow with time-bounded phases.

title: Interface view drops new fields during version upgrade
source_title: DAML Upgrade Pattern - Interface Version Compatibility
description: When a template is upgraded to include new fields, the interface view implementation still uses the old record shape, silently dropping the new field. Consumers fetching via the interface receive incomplete data with no error indication.
recommendation: Use Optional fields in the interface view for backward-compatible extensions, or version the interface itself.

code_snippets.label: View drops metadata on V2 contracts

oracle.description: Interface views must include all semantically relevant template fields.
oracle.static_signal: Template field not mapped in interface view record
oracle.symbolic_property: forall f. semantic_field(template, f) => represented_in(view, f)

notes: Related to CANTON-REL-01 and DAML-REL-01 (LF version issues).

title: Controller party not listed as observer on contract
source_title: DAML Visibility Pattern - Observer Requirement for Controllers
description: A party designated as a choice controller is not listed in the observer clause. In DAML, a party must be able to see a contract to exercise choices on it. Without observer status, the controller cannot discover or interact with the contract through the ledger API.
recommendation: Always include choice controllers in the observer (or signatory) clause of the template.

oracle.description: Every choice controller must be a stakeholder (signatory or observer) of the contract.
oracle.static_signal: Choice controller party not in signatory/observer clause
oracle.symbolic_property: forall c. controller(choice, c) => observer(template, c) || signatory(template, c)

notes: One of the most mechanically detectable DAML bugs.

title: LP token supply can be created or consumed from a different pool
description: LP (Liquidity Provider) tokens associated with one liquidity pool can be created or consumed in the context of a different pool. This breaks the fundamental invariant that LP token supply should be scoped to a specific pool, enabling cross-pool token manipulation that could lead to unauthorized liquidity extraction or inflation of LP token supply.
recommendation: Enforce that LP token operations (mint/burn) validate the pool identity, ensuring tokens can only be created or consumed within their originating pool.

oracle.description: LP token mint/burn operations do not validate that the pool context matches the LP token's associated pool.
oracle.static_signal: Token mint/burn operation that does not assert token.poolId == currentPool.id before modifying supply
oracle.symbolic_property: forall op in {mint_lp, burn_lp}: op.token.poolId == op.pool.id

notes: Critical severity - could lead to direct fund loss. Classic authorization/scoping bug in multi-pool AMM designs.

title: User controlled transfer fee parameter enables unauthorized token overpayment
description: The transfer fee parameter is user-controlled and not validated against the authoritative fee configuration. A user can specify an arbitrary fee value in their transaction, potentially causing unauthorized overpayment of tokens to the fee recipient or manipulation of the fee distribution mechanism.
recommendation: Validate the transfer fee parameter against the authoritative fee configuration stored on-ledger, rejecting any user-supplied fee that does not match the expected value.

oracle.description: User-supplied fee parameter is not validated against the on-ledger fee configuration before being applied.
oracle.static_signal: Choice parameter for fee/amount that is used in calculations without asserting it matches a fetched on-ledger authoritative value
oracle.symbolic_property: forall tx: tx.fee == fetch(feeConfig).currentFee

notes: Critical severity - direct financial impact. Classic 'trust user input' bug applied to fee parameters.

title: Allocation execution window is more restrictive than the settlement model
description: The allocation execution window is more restrictive than what the settlement model allows. Legitimate settlement operations may fail because the allocation's time constraints are tighter than the settlement's overall timeline, creating unnecessary execution failures.
recommendation: Align the allocation execution window with the settlement model's timing constraints to avoid rejecting valid operations.

oracle.description: Allocation time window is stricter than the settlement model's window, causing valid operations to be rejected.
oracle.static_signal: Time window validation in allocation that uses stricter bounds than the parent settlement's time window
oracle.symbolic_property: allocation.executionWindow is subset of settlement.executionWindow

notes: Temporal window misalignment is a recurring DAML audit finding.

title: Multiple liquidity pools can be created for the same instrument pair
description: The pool creation logic does not enforce uniqueness of instrument pairs, allowing multiple liquidity pools to be created for the same pair of instruments. This fragments liquidity, confuses users, and may lead to arbitrage exploitation across duplicate pools.
recommendation: Enforce uniqueness of instrument pairs when creating new pools, rejecting creation requests for pairs that already have an active pool.

oracle.description: Pool creation does not validate that no existing pool already covers the same instrument pair.
oracle.static_signal: Create operation for a pool-like template that does not check for existing contracts with the same key fields (instrument pair)
oracle.symbolic_property: forall p1 p2: active(p1) AND active(p2) AND p1.instrumentPair == p2.instrumentPair => p1 == p2

notes: DAML has contract keys for uniqueness enforcement, but they may not have been used here.

title: Swap activity is not recorded for featured rewards
description: Swap transactions do not record activity needed for featured reward calculations. Users who perform swaps may not receive the rewards they are entitled to because the system has no record of their swap activity for reward distribution purposes.
recommendation: Record swap activity in a form that can be queried for reward calculations, either as events or as state updates to reward-tracking contracts.

oracle.description: Swap execution does not emit or record activity needed for reward calculation.
oracle.static_signal: Swap choice body that does not create an activity/event record or update a reward-tracking contract

notes: Business logic completeness issue. The swap executes correctly but lacks side-effect recording for incentive mechanisms.

title: Settlement deadlines allow logically invalid execution windows
description: Settlement deadlines can be configured in a way that creates logically invalid execution windows (e.g., zero duration or negative duration windows). This can cause all settlement attempts to fail or create impossible-to-meet timing requirements.
recommendation: Validate settlement deadline configuration to ensure execution windows have positive, meaningful duration.

oracle.description: Settlement deadline validation allows degenerate configurations where the execution window is zero or negative.
oracle.static_signal: Deadline validation using <= instead of < between start and end times, or missing validation entirely
oracle.symbolic_property: forall s: settlement: s.executionStart < s.executionEnd

notes: Near-identical pattern to . Temporal boundary validation is a cross-cutting DAML bug pattern.

title: Zero liquidity pool creation due to decimal quantization
description: Due to decimal precision and quantization behavior, it is possible to create a liquidity pool with effectively zero liquidity. Rounding during the initial deposit calculation can reduce the effective deposit to zero while still creating the pool contract, leaving an empty but active pool.
recommendation: Validate that the effective deposited amounts are greater than zero after all rounding/quantization, rejecting pool creation if the result would be a zero-liquidity pool.

oracle.description: Pool creation does not validate that effective liquidity after decimal rounding is greater than zero.
oracle.static_signal: Create/ensure clause that checks amount > 0 on raw input but not on the post-quantization result
oracle.symbolic_property: forall pool: active(pool) => pool.effectiveLiquidity > 0

notes: Numeric precision issues are common in financial DAML contracts. DAML uses fixed-point Decimal with limited precision.

title: Exact Decimal comparisons on computed values
description: The code uses exact equality (==) to compare Decimal values that are the result of arithmetic computations. Due to rounding behavior in fixed-point arithmetic, computed values may differ by tiny amounts that are functionally equivalent but fail exact equality checks, causing spurious assertion failures.
recommendation: Use tolerance-based comparison or ensure that all compared values go through the same rounding pipeline before comparison.

oracle.description: Exact equality comparison on computed Decimal values may fail due to rounding differences.
oracle.static_signal: Use of == on Decimal values that are results of arithmetic operations (multiplication, division) rather than direct field access
oracle.symbolic_property: forall a b: computed_decimal(a) == computed_decimal(b) is unreliable; use |a - b| < epsilon

notes: DAML uses fixed-point 38-digit Decimal, not floating point. But division still produces rounding, making exact comparison unreliable on computed values.

title: Requests and relationships cannot be explicitly revoked once created
description: Once requests or operational relationships (e.g., AMMRules) are created, there is no explicit mechanism to revoke or cancel them. This means that even if circumstances change (e.g., a party should no longer have access), the existing contracts remain active indefinitely.
recommendation: Add explicit revocation/cancellation choices that allow authorized parties to archive requests and relationships when they are no longer needed.

oracle.description: Templates lack explicit choices for cancellation/revocation, relying only on the implicit Archive which may not have the right authorization model.
oracle.static_signal: Template without any consuming choice that acts as cancellation/revocation; only the implicit Archive is available
oracle.symbolic_property: exists choice c on AMMRules: exercising c archives the contract AND c is authorized by the appropriate governance party

notes: (irrevocable observer). Missing revocation is a recurring DAML design issue.

title: Fee updates can silently disable swaps
description: Updating fee parameters can result in a configuration that silently disables all swap operations. Certain fee values (e.g., 100% fee, or fee configurations that make swaps economically impossible) are not rejected during the update, leading to a state where the AMM appears active but no swaps can succeed.
recommendation: Validate fee parameter updates to ensure the resulting configuration still allows swap operations, rejecting updates that would effectively disable the AMM.

oracle.description: Fee update does not validate that the new fee configuration still permits functional swap operations.
oracle.static_signal: Fee update choice that sets fee parameters without bounds checking (e.g., fee < 100%, fee >= 0)
oracle.symbolic_property: forall feeUpdate(newFee): newFee < 1.0 AND newFee >= 0.0

notes: Interesting pattern: a valid-looking administrative action that silently breaks core functionality.

title: Lack of explicit venue and vault consistency checks when creating pool requests
description: When creating pool requests, there are no explicit checks to ensure that the specified venue and vault are consistent (e.g., that the vault belongs to the specified venue). This could allow pool creation with mismatched venue/vault combinations, leading to operational issues.
recommendation: Add validation that the venue and vault referenced in pool creation requests are consistent with each other.

oracle.description: Pool creation does not validate consistency between venue and vault references.
oracle.static_signal: Create/exercise that references multiple related entities (venue, vault) without asserting their relationship
oracle.symbolic_property: forall req: poolRequest: req.vault.venue == req.venue

notes: Common pattern: multiple related entity references without cross-validation of their relationships.

title: Cancellation reason for pool creation requests is not persisted
description: When pool creation requests are cancelled, the reason for cancellation is not persisted on-ledger. This reduces the auditability of the system and makes it difficult to retrospectively understand why certain pool creation attempts were rejected.
recommendation: Persist the cancellation reason either as a field in a cancellation event or as metadata in the archive operation.

oracle.description: Cancellation operation does not record the reason, reducing audit trail quality.
oracle.static_signal: Archive/cancel choice that does not create an event or record with a reason/rationale field

notes: Low benchmark value. (indistinguishable audit records).

title: Remainder holding not returned on partial split
description: When a partial split is performed on a holding, the remainder portion is not returned to the original owner. This results in the remainder being effectively lost or orphaned, creating an asset accounting discrepancy.
recommendation: Ensure that the split operation always returns the remainder holding to the original owner or accounts for it in the output.

oracle.description: Split operation does not return the remainder holding, causing asset loss.
oracle.static_signal: Split function that creates the requested portion but does not create or return a remainder contract when input amount > requested amount
oracle.symbolic_property: forall split(h, amount): amount < h.amount => exists remainder: remainder.amount == h.amount - amount

notes: Remediation: Acknowledged (not fixed). This is a significant asset safety issue even at informational severity.

title: Receiver party silently replaced after allocation consolidation
description: After allocation consolidation (merging multiple allocations), the receiver party may be silently replaced with a different party than the original intended receiver. This could redirect assets to an unintended recipient without explicit authorization from the original parties.
recommendation: Validate that the receiver party remains consistent through allocation consolidation and reject any consolidation that would change the receiver.

oracle.description: Allocation consolidation can silently change the receiver party, potentially redirecting assets.
oracle.static_signal: Merge/consolidation function that takes receiver from one of the inputs without asserting all inputs have the same receiver
oracle.symbolic_property: forall consolidate(a1, a2): result.receiver == a1.receiver AND a1.receiver == a2.receiver

notes: Potentially high-impact if exploitable. Silent party replacement during consolidation is a classic merge-logic bug.

title: Cross-role delegation usage not prevented at settlement signing
description: A delegation granted for one role can be used at settlement signing for a different role. The settlement signing logic does not validate that the delegation being exercised matches the role context of the settlement, allowing cross-role privilege escalation through delegation reuse.
recommendation: Validate at settlement signing time that the delegation being exercised is scoped to the correct role and reject cross-role usage.

oracle.description: Settlement signing does not validate that the delegation's role matches the role required for the signing operation.
oracle.static_signal: Delegation exercise that does not check delegation.role == expected_role before granting authority
oracle.symbolic_property: forall sign(delegation, settlement): delegation.role == settlement.required_role

notes: Classic authorization bug: insufficient scope validation on delegated authority.

title: Optional deadline produces misleading assertion messages
description: When a deadline field is Optional and set to None, the assertion message produced is misleading or confusing. The error text may reference a deadline value that does not exist, making debugging difficult.
recommendation: Produce clear assertion messages that correctly handle the Optional case, distinguishing between 'no deadline set' and 'deadline violated'.

oracle.description: Assertion on Optional deadline produces confusing messages when the deadline is None.
oracle.static_signal: assertMsg on Optional Time field that does not handle the None case with a distinct message

notes: Misleading error messages are a recurring theme in DAML audits.

title: Inconsistent state management when locking all holdings including remainder
description: When locking all holdings including the remainder in an order workflow, state management becomes inconsistent. The remainder holding may be incorrectly handled during the lock operation, leading to state divergence between the locked holdings and the expected contract state. This could result in asset accounting errors or failed subsequent operations.
recommendation: Ensure consistent state tracking when the lock operation encompasses all holdings including remainder, so that the post-lock state accurately reflects the full set of locked assets.

oracle.description: The state after locking all holdings (including remainder) does not accurately reflect the expected locked asset set.
oracle.static_signal: Lock operation that handles remainder holdings differently from regular holdings; missing edge case when remainder is included in the lock set
oracle.symbolic_property: sum(locked_holdings) + remainder == total_pre_lock_balance

notes: Highest severity finding in the Temple audit. Full technical details in downloadable PDF report.

title: Missing expiration validation during order matching
description: The order matching logic does not validate whether orders have expired before proceeding with the match. Expired orders can be matched against live orders, leading to invalid settlements or wasted resources on orders that should no longer be active.
recommendation: Add expiration time validation at the point of order matching to reject expired orders before they enter the matching workflow.

oracle.description: The order matching function does not check order expiration time against current ledger time before proceeding.
oracle.static_signal: Order matching function that fetches/exercises orders without checking an expiration/deadline field against getTime
oracle.symbolic_property: forall match(o1, o2): ledger_time < o1.expiresAt AND ledger_time < o2.expiresAt

notes: Similar pattern to (temporal check missing). Common DAML bug: forgetting to validate time constraints at key state transitions.

title: Insufficient validation of context data enabling potential denial of service
description: Context data passed into the order workflow is not sufficiently validated, potentially allowing malformed or adversarial context inputs to cause denial of service conditions. This could manifest as failed transactions, resource exhaustion, or unexpected workflow interruptions.
recommendation: Add comprehensive validation of context data at entry points before it is used in downstream processing.

oracle.description: Context data is used in workflow operations without adequate validation of its structure and values.
oracle.static_signal: Function accepting context/metadata parameter that is used in downstream operations without validation assertions

notes: Remediation status: Risk Accepted (not fixed). This may indicate the team considers the DoS risk acceptable given their deployment constraints.

title: Unsafe list access assumptions on transfer operation results
description: The code makes unsafe assumptions about the length of lists returned from transfer operations, using head/tail or index access without checking that the list has the expected number of elements. If a transfer returns fewer results than expected (e.g., due to edge cases), this would cause a runtime crash.
recommendation: Replace unsafe list access with safe pattern matching or explicit length checks before indexing.

oracle.description: List access (head, !!, tail) is used on transfer results without length validation, risking runtime errors on empty or short lists.
oracle.static_signal: Use of 'head', '!!', or 'tail' on a list without prior length check or pattern match; especially on return values from external operations
oracle.symbolic_property: forall r in transferResults: length(r) >= expected_count before access

notes: Common DAML/Haskell bug: using partial functions (head, !!) on potentially empty lists. Easily detectable by static analysis.

title: Order matching does not enforce FIFO ordering
description: The order matching logic does not enforce first-in-first-out (FIFO) ordering when selecting orders for matching. Orders may be matched in an arbitrary order rather than by their submission time, potentially causing unfair execution where later orders are matched before earlier ones.
recommendation: Enforce FIFO ordering in the matching algorithm by sorting or prioritizing orders based on their creation/submission timestamp.

oracle.description: Order matching does not sort or prioritize by creation time, violating expected FIFO semantics for fair order execution.
oracle.static_signal: Matching function that iterates over a list of orders without sorting by timestamp/creation time field
oracle.symbolic_property: forall o1, o2: matched(o1) AND waiting(o2) AND o2.createdAt < o1.createdAt => contradiction

notes: Risk Accepted. Enforcing FIFO on a distributed ledger is non-trivial; the team may rely on off-chain ordering. Interesting benchmark for specification-level properties.

title: Inconsistent deadline handling for re-locked order allocations
description: When order allocations are re-locked (e.g., after a partial fill or workflow retry), the deadline/expiration handling is inconsistent with the initial lock. The re-locked allocation may have a different effective deadline than expected, potentially allowing operations outside the intended time window.
recommendation: Ensure that re-locked allocations inherit or properly recalculate deadlines consistently with the original allocation constraints.

oracle.description: Re-locked allocations have different deadline semantics than initially locked ones.
oracle.static_signal: Re-lock or re-create operation that sets deadline/expiry fields differently from the initial create path
oracle.symbolic_property: relock(alloc).deadline is_consistent_with initial_lock(alloc).deadline

notes: Combines temporal and lifecycle patterns. Relevant to any DAML workflow that involves re-locking or re-creating contracts.

title: Non-deterministic maker-taker assignment for concurrent orders
description: When two concurrent orders are matched, the assignment of maker vs taker roles is non-deterministic. Depending on the order of contract processing, either party could be assigned either role. This affects fee calculations, priority rules, and any logic that depends on the maker/taker distinction.
recommendation: Introduce deterministic rules for maker/taker assignment (e.g., based on order creation time, price priority, or explicit role designation).

oracle.description: Maker/taker role assignment depends on non-deterministic execution ordering rather than deterministic criteria.
oracle.static_signal: Role assignment logic that depends on the order of input parameters without explicit sorting/comparison criteria
oracle.symbolic_property: forall o1 o2: match(o1, o2).maker == match(o2, o1).maker (determinism)

notes: Relevant to any DAML matching engine. Non-determinism in DAML is a subtle issue since the ledger model is otherwise deterministic.

title: Misleading choice name suggesting validation-only operation
description: A choice name suggests it performs only validation (e.g., 'Validate' or 'Check'), but the choice actually performs state-changing operations beyond validation. This can mislead developers and auditors into treating the choice as side-effect-free when it is not.
recommendation: Rename the choice to accurately reflect its actual behavior, distinguishing between validation-only and state-modifying operations.

oracle.description: A choice named to suggest validation-only behavior actually performs state mutations (create/archive/exercise).
oracle.static_signal: Choice with name containing 'Validate', 'Check', or 'Verify' that contains create/archive/exercise calls in its body

notes: Low benchmark value but detectable by static analysis: check choice names against their actual side effects.
